name: Keyhole (Pyraminx)
description: A beginner-to-intermediate Pyraminx method that uses a keyhole technique. The solver leaves one position open (the 'keyhole') while inserting edges one at a time, then solves the final edges. This method is very intuitive and requires minimal algorithms, making it a popular choice for those transitioning from absolute beginner methods. The keyhole concept is easy to grasp and applies fundamental puzzle-solving logic.

steps.name: Orient Tips
steps.description: Twist all four tips to match their adjacent center colors. This trivial step can be done at any time during the solve.
tips: Some solvers prefer tips first, others do them last

steps.name: Solve First Two Edges Using Keyhole
steps.description: Choose a bottom edge position as the keyhole (open slot). Solve two of the three bottom edges by using the open keyhole position to cycle edges without disturbing already-solved pieces. Insert edges one at a time, always keeping one slot open as a working space.
tips: Pick the easiest edge to place first
Use the open slot strategically — it allows free movement

steps.name: Solve Remaining Edges
steps.description: Close the keyhole and solve the remaining edges. This involves placing the last bottom edge and simultaneously ensuring the top edges are correct. A few simple algorithms or intuitive moves handle the final cases.
tips: Recognize whether remaining edges need cycling or flipping
Learn the small set of finishing algorithms
With practice, this step can be done quickly

pros: Keyhole concept is easy to understand
Good stepping stone to L4E and other advanced methods
cons: Higher move count than L4E or Oka
Not used competitively at high levels
